title: Correlation Basics: Definitions, Applications, and Terminology

intro.why: Correlation is the quiet variable behind almost every risk number a firm produces: it determines how much diversification a portfolio really delivers, how structured credit products are priced, how exposed a hedge is to its own counterparty defaulting at the worst possible moment, and how a calm market can suddenly turn systemic when everything starts moving together. This chapter builds a working vocabulary for correlation risk — the danger that correlations themselves move against you — and then traces that idea through investing, trading, regulatory capital, and the 2007-2009 financial crisis. The portfolio mean/variance/covariance/correlation math introduced here is the same machinery sitting inside every variance-covariance VaR model from earlier readings, so this chapter is really about naming and generalizing something you've already been using.
intro.content:
The six learning outcomes radiate outward from one foundational idea — the correlation coefficient — into its applications and consequences:

  1. Financial Correlation Risk and Its Applications — the foundation: what correlation risk is, the static-vs-dynamic distinction, wrong-way risk, and the Markowitz portfolio math (mean, variance, covariance, correlation) that everything else builds on.
  2. Correlation and the 2007-2009 Financial Crisis — the cautionary tale: what happens at a systemic scale when copula correlation assumptions used to price CDOs turn out to be wrong exactly when it matters most.
  3. Correlation Swaps — a trading structure that bets directly on the realized value of the correlation coefficient itself versus a fixed rate agreed today.
  4. Correlation Risk and the VaR Capital Charge — a mechanical link: how the correlation assumption embedded in a covariance matrix moves portfolio VaR, and therefore moves the regulatory capital charge.
  5. Correlation Risk, Market Risk, and Credit Risk — correlation's dual role: populating the covariance matrix behind VaR/ES on one side, and driving default correlation across a loan book on the other.
  6. Correlation Risk, Systemic Risk, and Concentration Risk — zooming out to the system level (correlations spiking together in a crisis) and down to the portfolio level (the concentration ratio as a simple diversification proxy).

Keep an eye on the throughline: a single correlation coefficient — one number between -1 and +1 — quietly determines diversification benefits, derivative prices, VaR figures, capital charges, and systemic fragility, and the entire 2007-2009 crisis can be read as a story about that one number being assumed wrong at scale.

los.lo: LO 7.a — Describe financial correlation risk and the areas in which it appears in finance
los.title: Financial Correlation Risk and Its Applications
los.why: This LO sets up the vocabulary and the math that every other LO in this chapter leans on: what correlation risk actually is, the static-vs-dynamic distinction that recurs throughout, the wrong-way-risk concept that reappears in the crisis and credit-risk discussions, and the portfolio mean/variance/covariance/correlation framework that underlies the VaR capital charge LO. Everything downstream is an application or consequence of the ideas introduced here.
los.keyIdea: Correlation risk is the chance of losing money purely because the relationship between two variables shifts — even if neither variable individually does anything unusual.
los.content:
What correlation risk is

Correlation risk is a financial loss that arises from a change in how two or more variables move together, rather than from a change in either variable on its own. A classic example: interest rates and commodity prices often move in opposite directions, so a rise in rates can hurt commodity positions even though nothing changed in commodity supply or demand. During the 2012 Greek sovereign debt troubles, Mexican bonds — unrelated to Greece — fell in value simply because they happened to move with Greek bonds, hurting investors who may not even have been tracking that relationship.

The 2007-2009 financial crisis is the textbook case at global scale: assets that had historically shown low or negative correlation suddenly all moved down together, destroying the diversification many portfolios were relying on.

Correlation risk isn't limited to financial instruments — macro and political variables link up too: a weaker currency in one country can hurt exporters elsewhere (a 2012 weak-euro episode hurt some U.S. exporters); weak U.S. growth ripples out to depress demand for Asian and European exporters; political unrest that pushes oil prices higher can reduce airline travel.

Static vs. dynamic correlation

Correlation measures split into two broad categories:

  • Static correlation measures are a fixed snapshot for a given period — examples include the correlation numbers inside a VaR covariance matrix, copula-based correlation assumptions used to price CDO tranches, and binomial default-correlation models. A static measure can go stale fast: accurate “for this period” may say nothing about the next.
  • Dynamic correlation measures try to track how the relationship evolves over time — pairs-trading strategies, deterministic time-varying correlation models, and models where correlation itself is a random (stochastic) process. More realistic, but more complex to estimate and apply.

Wrong-way risk in a CDS

Suppose an investor holding government bonds buys CDS protection from a large bank against that government defaulting (zero recovery, no accrued interest, for simplicity). A new correlation question arises: how correlated is the protection seller's default probability with the reference issuer's default probability? If positively correlated, the investor faces wrong-way risk (WWR) — protection is most likely to fail exactly when it's most needed. Higher positive correlation between seller and reference entity therefore lowers the fair CDS spread, since the protection is worth less.

The relationship between correlation and the CDS spread can be nonmonotonic: at very high negative correlation, the spread can tick up slightly, because strong negative correlation implies at most one party is likely to default — if the reference entity defaults while the seller stays solvent, the investor is made whole; if instead the seller defaults while the reference entity doesn't, the investor must replace the protection, likely at a higher cost if the reference entity's own credit has also weakened.

Five areas where correlation risk matters

The reading groups applications into five areas: investments, trading, risk management, global markets, and regulation. The remaining LOs in this chapter dig into several of these individually (trading via multi-asset options and correlation swaps, risk management via the VaR capital charge, global markets via the 2007-2009 crisis, and the market/credit/systemic/concentration linkages).

Portfolio mean, variance, covariance, and correlation

Modern portfolio theory (Markowitz, early 1950s) established that the relationship between assets' returns — not just their individual risk and return — determines how much risk reduction diversification can deliver.

  • Portfolio mean return for a two-asset portfolio is just the weighted average of the two assets' average returns.
  • Variance of each asset: square each period's deviation from that asset's average return, sum, divide by n − 1.
  • Covariance follows the same pattern, but multiplies the paired deviations of the two assets in each period before summing and dividing by n − 1. Same-direction moves (both deviations positive or both negative) contribute positively; opposite-direction moves contribute negatively — so a negative covariance signals the assets tend to move in opposite directions.
  • Correlation coefficient standardizes covariance by dividing by the product of the two standard deviations, producing a value always between -1 and +1 — directly comparable across any pair of assets regardless of their individual volatility scales.
Worked example. A small sample of historical returns for assets X and Y gives: sum of squared deviations for X = 0.7079 → Var(X) = 0.7079/4 = 0.1770 → σ_X ≈ 0.4207; σ_Y ≈ 0.4068 (analogous calculation); sum of cross-products of deviations = -0.5135 → Cov(X,Y) = -0.5135/4 = -0.1284. So ρ_XY = -0.1284 / (0.4207 × 0.4068) ≈ -0.7501 — a fairly strong tendency for X and Y to move in opposite directions.

Portfolio standard deviation and the return/risk ratio

For a two-asset portfolio, the portfolio standard deviation depends on each asset's variance, each asset's weight, and the covariance (equivalently, correlation) between them. Holding everything else fixed, a lower (more negative) correlation produces a lower portfolio standard deviation — the mathematical expression of diversification.

Markowitz also emphasized a return/risk ratio — average return divided by standard deviation — for comparing portfolios on a risk-adjusted basis.

Worked example (equal weights, w_X = w_Y = 0.5). Using μ_X = 0.3019, μ_Y = 0.2032, σ_X = 0.4207, σ_Y = 0.4068, ρ_XY = -0.7501: μ_P = 0.2526 and σ_P ≈ 0.1464, giving a return/risk ratio of 0.2526 / 0.1464 ≈ 1.725 (about 172.5%). More generally, the lower the correlation between the two assets, the higher this ratio: a correlation around -0.9 can push it above 250%, while a correlation around +0.9 can pull it down to roughly 50%.

Correlation in multi-asset options

Some option contracts pay off based on the relationship between two underlyings rather than the level of a single asset. For most of these “correlation options,” lower correlation between the underlyings increases the option's value — if two assets aren't closely linked, it's more likely one moves up substantially while the other moves down or stays flat, widening the gap that many of these payoffs reward. The one notable exception: an option whose payoff depends on the minimum of two asset prices works the opposite way — lower correlation raises the chance that at least one asset performs poorly, which drags the “minimum” payoff down, so lower correlation reduces that option's value.

The exchange option pays max(0, S2 − S1) — the holder benefits whenever asset 2 ends up worth more than asset 1. Its value is driven by the volatility of the ratio S2/S1, which depends on both individual volatilities and the correlation between S1 and S2. If S1 and S2 are highly positively correlated, the ratio S2/S1 barely moves, the spread stays narrow, and the option's value approaches zero; as correlation falls, the ratio becomes more volatile, the spread is more likely to widen, and the option becomes more valuable.

The quanto option lets a domestic investor gain exposure to a foreign asset while locking in a fixed exchange rate for converting the payoff back to domestic currency, stripping out currency risk. The same rule applies: lower correlation between the foreign asset and the exchange rate produces a higher quanto price. Intuition for a quanto call on a foreign equity index: if the index and exchange rate are negatively correlated, a rising index (a deep-in-the-money call, so the seller owes a large foreign-currency payoff) tends to coincide with a weakening foreign currency — the seller needs more units of foreign currency to meet the fixed-rate conversion, an added hedging cost that gets priced into the option. So lower (or more negative) correlation between the index and the exchange rate means a higher quanto price — consistent with the general pattern.
los.formula:
Portfolio mean: mu_P = w_X * mu_X + w_Y * mu_Y
Variance: Var(X) = sigma_X^2 = sum[(X_t - mu_X)^2] / (n - 1)
Covariance: Cov(X,Y) = sum[(X_t - mu_X)(Y_t - mu_Y)] / (n - 1)
Correlation: rho_XY = cov_XY / (sigma_X * sigma_Y)
Portfolio SD: sigma_P = sqrt(w_X^2*sigma_X^2 + w_Y^2*sigma_Y^2 + 2*w_X*w_Y*rho_XY*sigma_X*sigma_Y)
Return/risk ratio = mu_P / sigma_P
Exchange option implied vol: sigma_E = sqrt(sigma_1^2 + sigma_2^2 - 2*rho_12*sigma_1*sigma_2)
takeaways: Correlation risk is loss from a changed relationship between variables, not from either variable alone — and it spans financial, macro, and political variables.
Static correlation measures are snapshots (VaR covariance matrices, CDO copula assumptions) and can go stale fast; dynamic measures track evolving relationships at the cost of complexity.
Wrong-way risk: positive correlation between a CDS protection seller's and reference entity's default probabilities means protection fails exactly when it's needed most.

Lower (more negative) correlation between two assets lowers portfolio standard deviation and raises the return/risk ratio — and for most correlation options (except minimum-of-two payoffs), lower correlation raises option value.

los.lo: LO 7.b — Explain how correlation contributed to the global financial crisis of 2007-2009
los.title: Correlation and the 2007-2009 Financial Crisis
los.why: This LO is the cautionary case study for the static-correlation idea introduced in LO 7.a: it shows concretely what happens when an entire financial system relies on copula correlation assumptions that break down at the worst possible time, and sets up the systemic-risk discussion in LO 7.f, where correlation spikes are examined more generally.
los.keyIdea: The 2007-2009 crisis shows what happens when copula correlation assumptions used to price CDO tranches turn out to be wrong exactly when the system needs them most.
los.content:
The setup

Several conditions combined to leave the system unusually exposed to correlation risk: a post-dot-com period of low credit spreads, low rates, and low volatility encouraged risk-taking; an overheated housing market encouraged households to take on debt against inflated property values; new structured products (CDOs, CPDOs, CDSs) made it easy to package and distribute real-estate lending exposure, fueling further speculation; and rating agencies, risk managers, and regulators broadly underestimated how much leverage borrowers and institutions were accumulating. The trigger was defaults in subprime mortgages.

The copula correlation model and CDO tranches

A central technical failure involved the copula correlation model, used to estimate default correlations among the many assets inside a CDO. A typical CDO referencing 125 credits requires n(n-1)/2 = 125 × 124 / 2 = 7,750 pairwise correlations — an enormous estimation problem the copula approach was meant to make tractable.

CDOs split the credit pool into tranches by seniority: the equity tranche (riskiest, absorbing roughly the first 3% of losses), the mezzanine tranche (next, roughly 3%-7%), and more senior tranches above that. A number of large hedge funds went long the equity tranche and short the mezzanine tranche, betting gains on one side would offset losses on the other — a bet that depended on the copula model's assumptions about how the two tranches' spreads would move relative to each other.

The May 2005 trigger: GM and Ford downgrades

In May 2005, Ford and GM bonds were downgraded to junk status, forcing large institutional holders (pension funds, insurers) restricted from holding non-investment-grade debt to sell, causing sharp price declines independent of actual default probability. This also illustrates a broader pattern: bonds within the same credit-quality tier tend to be more highly correlated with each other than bonds across tiers.

After the downgrades, the equity tranche spread widened sharply — hurting the long-equity-tranche hedge funds. At the same time, correlations within the investment-grade CDO universe declined, pushing the mezzanine tranche spread down — hurting the same funds on their short mezzanine positions. The funds lost on both legs of what was supposed to be a hedged trade, because the copula-implied relationship between the two tranches' spreads didn't behave as assumed.

From subprime defaults to a global crisis

The CDO market grew from roughly $64 billion in 2003 to roughly $455 billion in 2006, much of it backed by residential mortgages. Home prices stagnated starting in 2006, mortgage defaults followed, and by 2007 real estate was in outright decline. Because CDOs were so closely tied to mortgages and held globally, the collapse propagated worldwide into equity and commodity markets.

As the crisis spread, correlations rose sharply across multiple dimensions at once: correlations among stocks rose as equity markets fell together, and default correlations within CDO and bond markets rose as creditworthiness was questioned broadly.

Normally, a rise in default correlation lowers the equity tranche spread (other things equal, raising its value). But during the crisis, the probability of default itself rose so dramatically that it overwhelmed this correlation effect and dragged down every CDO tranche — including AAA tranches, some of which lost around 20% of their value once it was clear the lower tranches could no longer absorb losses first. Institutions with heavy leverage in these “safe” senior tranches suffered the largest losses, with effective exposures on the order of 10-20 times the underlying invested amounts.

The CDS market and systemically important failures

The CDS market expanded from around $8 trillion (2004) to around $60 trillion (2007) in notional terms — functioning like insurance, transferring credit exposure to protection sellers who need to be financially sound enough to pay claims. One large global insurer had written roughly $500 billion of CDS protection with comparatively little reinsurance, leaving it severely exposed. One major investment bank entered bankruptcy in September 2008 with leverage of roughly 30 times — and an even larger effective exposure once its roughly 8,000 derivatives counterparties are considered.

Regulatory response

Regulators developed a new framework (commonly called Basel III), including new liquidity and leverage requirements. On the correlation modeling side, the crisis spurred Gaussian copula variants, credit valuation adjustment (CVA) methods that explicitly price counterparty correlations, and explicit modeling of wrong-way risk — the same WWR concept from LO 7.a.
los.formula:
Number of pairwise correlations for n assets = n(n-1) / 2
For n = 125 credits: 125 * 124 / 2 = 7,750 pairwise correlations
takeaways: A 125-credit CDO requires estimating 7,750 pairwise correlations (n(n-1)/2) — copula models were the shortcut for this enormous estimation problem.
The May 2005 Ford/GM downgrades hurt long-equity/short-mezzanine hedge funds on both legs, because copula-implied spread relationships broke down.
Bonds within the same credit-quality tier are more correlated with each other than bonds across tiers.
During the crisis, a system-wide rise in default probability overwhelmed the usual correlation-driven relationship, dragging down even AAA CDO tranches by around 20%.

los.lo: LO 7.c — Describe the structure, uses, and payoffs of a correlation swap
los.title: Correlation Swaps
los.why: Correlation swaps turn the abstract idea of "correlation risk" from LO 7.a into a tradeable, directly observable payoff — a clean illustration of how a single correlation number (realized correlation) can be the entire basis of a financial contract. They also connect to the multi-asset-options material folded into LO 7.a as another way to take a view on correlation.
los.keyIdea: A correlation swap pays the buyer notional × (realized correlation − fixed correlation), letting two parties bet directly on a basket's average pairwise correlation versus a rate agreed today.
los.content:
Structure

A correlation swap has two counterparties: the buyer agrees to pay a fixed correlation rate set at inception (e.g., 30%), and the seller receives that fixed rate. At maturity, the contract settles based on the difference between the realized correlation that actually occurred over the swap's life and the fixed rate agreed at the start. Realized correlation (sometimes called stochastic correlation, since it's unknown in advance) is computed from the actual pairwise correlations observed among the basket's assets.

Payoff

The buyer profits when realized correlation comes in above the fixed rate — the position's value to the buyer rises as realized correlation increases. The payoff to the buyer is the notional amount multiplied by (realized correlation − fixed rate). If realized correlation is below the fixed rate, this payoff is negative: the buyer loses and the seller gains the corresponding amount.

Realized correlation for a basket

For a basket of more than two assets, “realized correlation” is the simple average of all the pairwise correlation coefficients among the basket's assets, computed from each asset's actual log returns over the swap's life. For n assets there are n(n-1)/2 distinct pairs, and realized correlation averages the correlation coefficients across all of them.

Worked example. A buyer agrees to pay a fixed correlation rate of 0.20 on a $1,000,000 notional, one-year swap on a basket of three assets. At maturity, the three pairwise correlations of daily log returns are 0.60, 0.20, and 0.04.
Realized correlation = (0.60 + 0.20 + 0.04) / 3 = 0.28.
Buyer's payoff = $1,000,000 × (0.28 − 0.20) = $80,000.
Because realized correlation (0.28) exceeded the fixed rate (0.20), the buyer receives a positive $80,000 payoff.

Other ways to “buy correlation”

Two other structures achieve a similar economic exposure to correlation swaps:

  • Index vs. single-name call options. Buy calls on a broad equity index while selling calls on the individual constituent stocks. If correlation among constituents rises, the index call's implied volatility tends to rise more than the individual-stock calls' implied volatility (since index volatility is itself a function of constituent correlation), so the gain on the long index-call leg is expected to outweigh the loss on the short single-stock-call legs — effectively a long correlation trade.
  • Index vs. single-name variance swaps. Pay fixed on a variance swap on the index while receiving fixed on variance swaps on the individual constituents. A rise in constituent correlation increases the index's own variance; since the fixed-rate payer benefits when realized variance rises, this is again a long correlation exposure.
los.formula:
Realized correlation: rho_realized = [sum over pairs (i,j), i > j, of rho_(i,j)] / [n(n-1)/2]
Payoff to buyer = notional * (rho_realized - rho_fixed)
Example (n=3, pairwise rhos 0.60/0.20/0.04): rho_realized = (0.60+0.20+0.04)/3 = 0.28
Payoff = $1,000,000 * (0.28 - 0.20) = $80,000
takeaways: Buyer pays a fixed correlation rate; seller receives it; settlement is based on realized vs. fixed correlation at maturity.
Payoff to buyer = notional × (realized correlation − fixed rate) — positive only if realized correlation exceeds the fixed rate.
For baskets of n > 2 assets, realized correlation is the simple average of all n(n-1)/2 pairwise correlations of log returns.
Index-vs-single-name call options and index-vs-single-name variance swaps are alternative ways to take a long-correlation position.

los.lo: LO 7.d — Estimate the impact of different correlations between assets in the trading book on the VaR capital charge
los.title: Correlation Risk and the VaR Capital Charge
los.why: This LO is the most mechanical link in the chapter: it traces a single correlation assumption directly through the covariance matrix, into portfolio VaR, and out the other side as a regulatory capital number. It builds directly on the covariance and correlation math from LO 7.a, and the same direction-of-effect reappears in LO 7.e's discussion of correlation feeding VaR and ES generally.
los.keyIdea: Higher correlation between trading-book assets raises portfolio volatility, raises VaR, and therefore raises the Basel capital charge (3 × 10-day VaR) — all else equal.
los.content:
The variance-covariance (delta-normal) VaR formula

Under the variance-covariance approach, portfolio VaR over a chosen horizon comes from three ingredients: the portfolio's daily volatility (σ_P), a z-value for the desired confidence level from the standard normal distribution, and the square root of the number of trading days in the horizon (to scale one-day volatility up to a multi-day figure).

The portfolio's daily volatility itself comes from the covariance matrix together with each asset's dollar exposure: take the horizontal vector of dollar exposures, multiply by the covariance matrix, multiply that result by the same exposures as a vertical vector, and take the square root. Because the covariance matrix's diagonal holds each asset's variance and its off-diagonal holds covariances — and covariance is just correlation scaled by the two assets' standard deviations — correlation is embedded directly in this calculation.

Worked example: two-asset portfolio VaR

Portfolio: $8 million in asset A (daily σ_A = 1.5%), $4 million in asset B (daily σ_B = 2.0%), correlation ρ_AB = 0.6, confidence level 99% (z = 2.33), horizon = 10 days.
  1. Build the covariance matrix. Diagonal entries are each asset's own variance (σ²); the off-diagonal entry is Cov(A,B) = ρ_AB × σ_A × σ_B.
  2. Compute portfolio variance. Multiply the dollar-exposure vector ($8m, $4m) by the covariance matrix, then by the same exposure vector again, giving one-day portfolio variance in dollar terms.
  3. Take the square root for the one-day dollar standard deviation, scale by √10 for the 10-day horizon, and multiply by z = 2.33.
Result: 10-day 99% VaR ≈ $1,324,800. Interpretation: under the model's assumptions, a loss this large or larger over 10 days should occur only about 1% of the time — roughly once every four years at 250 trading days/year.

How correlation changes the answer

Re-evaluating the same portfolio (same dollar amounts, same individual volatilities) at a different assumed correlation between A and B changes VaR monotonically over the relevant range: the higher the correlation, the higher the portfolio VaR. A higher correlation means the two positions can't offset each other's moves as effectively, so the portfolio behaves more like one larger position and less like a diversified combination — raising volatility and VaR. A lower (or more negative) correlation has the opposite effect, since losses in one asset are more likely offset by gains in the other. This is the same diversification logic as the return/risk ratio in LO 7.a, just applied to a dollar VaR figure.

From VaR to the regulatory capital charge

The Basel Committee requires banks to hold regulatory capital against trading-book market risk based on portfolio VaR — specifically, at least 3 × the 10-day VaR.

Applying this to the worked example: capital charge = $1,324,800 × 3 = $3,974,400.

Because VaR increases with the correlation assumed between trading-book assets, the capital charge increases right along with it — a bank that assumes (or experiences) higher correlations among its positions must hold more capital, all else equal. This is one of the most direct, mechanical links in the entire chapter between a correlation assumption and a regulatory capital requirement.
los.formula:
VaR = sigma_P * alpha * sqrt(x)
sigma_P = sqrt(beta_h x C x beta_v)
(C = variance-covariance matrix; beta_h, beta_v = dollar exposure vectors)
Capital charge = 3 * (10-day VaR)
Example: sigma_A=1.5%, sigma_B=2.0%, rho_AB=0.6, alpha=2.33, x=10 -> VaR ~= $1,324,800
Capital charge = 3 * $1,324,800 = $3,974,400
takeaways: Correlation sits inside the off-diagonal entries of the covariance matrix (Cov = ρ × σ_A × σ_B), so it directly affects portfolio volatility σ_P.
Higher correlation between trading-book assets → higher σ_P → higher VaR → higher capital charge, monotonically.
The Basel capital charge is set at a minimum of 3 × the 10-day VaR.
The worked example (correlation 0.6, $8m/$4m exposures) gives a 10-day 99% VaR of ~$1,324,800 and a capital charge of ~$3,974,400.

los.lo: LO 7.e — Explain the role of correlation risk in market risk and credit risk
los.title: Correlation Risk, Market Risk, and Credit Risk
los.why: This LO generalizes LO 7.d's mechanical VaR/correlation link to expected shortfall and to the credit-risk side of the business, and sets up LO 7.f by introducing default correlation and the within-vs-across-industry pattern that the concentration-ratio worked examples build on.
los.keyIdea: Correlation isn't a standalone risk category — it's woven into the covariance matrix behind market-risk VaR/ES, and into the default correlation that governs how much real diversification a credit portfolio has.
los.content:
Correlation risk and market risk

Market risk is typically broken into interest rate, currency, equity price, and commodity price risk, most commonly summarized via value at risk (VaR). The covariance matrix of portfolio assets is a core VaR input (see LO 7.d), and correlation populates that matrix's off-diagonal entries — so any change in correlation directly changes VaR, even with no change in any individual asset's own volatility.

Expected shortfall (ES) focuses on tail severity — given a loss beyond the VaR threshold, how large is it likely to be? Like VaR, ES depends on the same covariance matrix, so it is equally exposed to correlation risk.

Put together: correlation risk in market risk is the risk that the correlations feeding the covariance matrix change over time, changing VaR and ES — independent of any change in individual asset volatilities.

Correlation risk and credit risk

On the credit side, risk managers focus on two related concepts:

  • Migration risk — a borrower's credit quality deteriorates (e.g., a rating downgrade), signaling higher eventual default probability and typically causing a mark-to-market loss for debt holders even before any actual default.
  • Default risk — the borrower fails to meet obligations outright.

Correlation enters most directly through default correlation — the degree to which multiple borrowers' defaults tend to happen together. A lower default correlation among borrowers means defaults are more spread out, giving the lender genuine diversification; a higher default correlation means one default raises the likelihood of others at the same time, concentrating losses.

The wrong-way-risk idea from LO 7.a is a special case of this same concept, applied to a single CDS counterparty/reference-entity pair rather than a whole loan portfolio: higher correlation between the counterparty's and reference entity's default probabilities raises the chance of a total loss.

Default correlation across and within industries

Empirical default data shows default correlations tend to be positive across most industry sectors — when conditions worsen broadly, defaults rise together across many industries. One exception: the energy sector has historically shown little correlation with the broader economy and tends to be comparatively resilient during downturns.

More importantly, default correlations tend to be higher within an industry than across industries — broad, systematic factors (overall economic conditions, industry-wide demand shocks) drive defaults far more than company-specific factors. If one major automaker defaults, that's more likely to signal trouble for other automakers (shared exposures to weak demand, high input costs) than to create an opportunity for survivors to absorb its market share.

The practical implication: diversifying loan exposure across industries — rather than concentrating within one — reduces default correlation among borrowers, reducing the chance a large share of the portfolio defaults together.

The term structure of default probabilities

Rating agencies publish default probabilities by both credit rating and time to maturity:

  • For investment-grade borrowers, default probability tends to increase gradually as the horizon lengthens — more time for adverse developments to occur.
  • For non-investment-grade (speculative/junk) borrowers, default probability is higher in the near term than later — a weak borrower's most dangerous period is the immediate future; if it survives that, its conditional default probability in later years actually declines.
takeaways: Correlation populates the off-diagonal entries of the covariance matrix behind both VaR and expected shortfall — a change in correlation changes both even if individual volatilities don't move.
Default correlation governs how much real diversification a loan portfolio has: lower default correlation spreads defaults out over time; higher default correlation concentrates them.
Default correlations are higher within an industry than across industries (energy being a notable low-correlation exception) — so cross-industry diversification reduces default correlation.
Investment-grade default probability rises with horizon length; non-investment-grade default probability is front-loaded (highest in the near term).

los.lo: LO 7.f — Relate correlation risk to systemic and concentration risk
los.title: Correlation Risk, Systemic Risk, and Concentration Risk
los.why: This LO closes the chapter by zooming out (systemic risk — correlation risk showing up everywhere at once) and back in (concentration risk — the same idea at the portfolio level), tying together the crisis narrative from LO 7.b and the default-correlation discussion from LO 7.e with a concrete set of worked expected-loss examples.
los.keyIdea: For a fixed total exposure, both a lower concentration ratio and a lower default correlation push the joint worst-case default probability — and therefore expected loss — down.
los.content:
Systemic risk

Systemic risk is the risk that the financial system as a whole could collapse — not just that one institution or asset class suffers losses, but that failure in one part cascades across many parts. A major investment bank's September 2008 bankruptcy filing is widely viewed as a key signal of how severe the crisis had become.

The scale of the 2007-2009 decline illustrates this vividly: from October 2007 to March 2009, a major U.S. equity benchmark fell more than 50%, and within a broad 500-stock index, only around 11 of 500 constituents actually rose — the remaining roughly 489 declined. The sectors among that small handful of risers — consumer staples, education, pharmaceuticals, and scattered names in agriculture, entertainment, energy, and auto-parts retail — share a common thread: demand for these goods and services holds up (or rises) during a downturn, making them comparatively “recession-resistant.”

Correlation spikes during the crisis

Studies of U.S. equity returns around 2007-2009 found average pairwise correlation among individual stocks rose dramatically during the worst part of the downturn (roughly August 2008-March 2009) — from a pre-crisis average of around 27% to over 50%. The consequence is the opposite of what investors want during a crisis: diversification benefits shrink exactly when needed most, because nearly all stocks move down together. The problem compounds further across asset classes — correlations between equities, bonds, and international equities also rose, so even multi-asset diversification offered less protection than usual.

Concentration risk and the concentration ratio

Concentration risk is the risk of loss from exposure concentrated among a small number of counterparties (or within a small number of related groups, like a single industry) rather than spread broadly. It's measured by the concentration ratio — one divided by the number of equally-sized exposures:

  • 100 equally sized loans to 100 different borrowers → concentration ratio = 1/100 = 0.01 (highly diversified).
  • A single loan to a single borrower → concentration ratio = 1/1 = 1.0 (maximally concentrated).

A lower concentration ratio means more diversification (lower concentration risk); a higher ratio means less diversification. If exposures are grouped by sector and within-sector defaults are correlated, a default by one borrower raises the likelihood of further defaults in that same sector — changes in the concentration ratio move together with changes in the portfolio's effective default correlation.

Worked examples: concentration ratio and expected loss

A hypothetical lender has a fixed total loan amount, each borrower has a 5% default probability, and LGD = 100% (default means complete loss of the outstanding balance).

One $5 million loan (concentration ratio = 1.0). Worst case is simply that the borrower defaults: expected loss = 0.05 × $5,000,000 = $250,000.
Two $2.5 million loans (concentration ratio = 0.5). The worst case is the joint probability both borrowers default, which depends on the default correlation between them (each borrower modeled as a Bernoulli variable with p = 0.05, σ ≈ 0.2179):
  • Default correlation = 1.0 → joint probability = 5% → expected loss = 0.05 × $5,000,000 = $250,000 — identical to the single $5m loan; perfectly correlated defaults across two loans provide no diversification benefit.
  • Default correlation = 0.5 → joint probability ≈ 0.02627 (2.627%) → expected loss ≈ $131,350.
  • Default correlation = 0 (independent) → joint probability = 0.05 × 0.05 = 0.0025 (0.25%) → expected loss = $12,500.
For a fixed total exposure and fixed individual default probabilities, lower default correlation between borrowers produces lower expected loss under the worst-case (joint default) scenario.
Three ~$1.67 million loans (concentration ratio = 0.333). The relevant worst case is now the joint probability that all three borrowers default simultaneously — smaller than the two-borrower joint probability for any given pairwise default correlation. The same directional takeaway holds: both a lower concentration ratio and a lower default correlation push the joint worst-case default probability — and expected loss — down. (The reading doesn't work through the full three-way joint probability formula; the key point is the direction of the relationship.)

Tying it together

Systemic risk and concentration risk are two views of the same phenomenon at different scales. Concentration risk asks: “if my small number of exposures are correlated, how much worse does my worst case get?” Systemic risk asks the same question at the scale of the entire financial system: “if everything becomes correlated at once, how much worse does the worst case get for everyone simultaneously?” The 2007-2009 crisis demonstrated both together — portfolios that looked diversified on paper (because components had historically shown low correlation) turned out to be far more concentrated in practice once correlations spiked across the board.
los.formula:
Concentration ratio = 1 / (number of equally sized exposures)
EL = P(joint default) * total exposure (LGD = 100%)
P(AB) = p^2 + rho_default * sigma_A * sigma_B, sigma = sqrt(p(1-p))
Example (p=0.05, sigma~=0.2179): rho=1.0 -> EL=$250,000; rho=0.5 -> EL~=$131,350; rho=0 -> EL=$12,500
takeaways: Systemic risk is correlation risk showing up everywhere at once — average pairwise equity correlation rose from ~27% pre-crisis to over 50% during 2008-2009.
During Oct 2007-Mar 2009, only ~11 of 500 index constituents rose — the survivors clustered in recession-resistant sectors (staples, education, pharma).
Concentration ratio = 1/(number of equal exposures); lower ratio = more diversification, and it moves together with the portfolio's effective default correlation.
Perfectly correlated defaults (ρ=1.0) across two loans give zero diversification benefit versus one big loan ($250,000 expected loss either way); independence (ρ=0) cuts expected loss to $12,500.
